title: N-Queens
description:
The **n-queens** puzzle is the problem of placing `n` queens on an `n x n` chessboard such that no two queens attack each other.

Given an integer `n`, return all distinct solutions to the **n-queens puzzle**. You may return the answer in **any order**.

Each solution contains a distinct board configuration of the n-queens' placement, where `'Q'` and `'.'` both indicate a queen and an empty space, respectively.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: n = 4
Output: [[".Q..","...Q","Q...","..Q."],["..Q.","Q...","...Q",".Q.."]]
Explanation: There exist two distinct solutions to the 4-queens puzzle.
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: n = 1
Output: [["Q"]]
Explanation: There is only one way to place a single queen on a 1x1 board.
```

examples.explanation: There are exactly two distinct solutions for placing 4 queens on a 4x4 board without conflicts.
A single queen on a 1x1 board is the only solution.

solution_approach: This is a classic backtracking problem. We place queens one row at a time, since each row must contain exactly one queen. For each row, we try placing a queen in each column and check whether it conflicts with any previously placed queen. Two queens conflict if they share the same column, the same main diagonal (row - col is constant), or the same anti-diagonal (row + col is constant). To check conflicts efficiently in O(1), we maintain three sets: one for occupied columns, one for occupied main diagonals (row - col values), and one for occupied anti-diagonals (row + col values). When we place a queen, we add to these sets; when we backtrack, we remove. If we successfully place queens in all n rows, we convert the board state to the required string format and add it to our results. The time complexity is O(n!) because in the first row we have n choices, in the second row at most n-1 (due to column constraint), and so on. The space complexity is O(n^2) for storing the board configurations.
hints: Place queens row by row. Since each row must have exactly one queen, you only need to decide which column to place it in for each row.
Two queens attack each other if they share the same column, the same diagonal (row - col), or the same anti-diagonal (row + col). Use sets to track which columns and diagonals are already occupied.
Backtrack: place a queen, mark its column and diagonals as occupied, recurse to the next row. If you reach row n, record the solution. On return, unmark the column and diagonals.
follow_up_questions: Instead of returning all solutions, how would you count the total number of distinct solutions efficiently (N-Queens II)?
Can you optimize the solution using bit manipulation to track columns and diagonals instead of sets?
